evidence: The live root TanStack app has a recognizable direction: routes and components import shared UI, auth, realtime, and API helpers; the helpers do not import routes or feature components.
The ASP.NET API inside each tree mostly keeps domain/data/auth/storage/realtime/features separated, with controllers consuming services or DbContext and domain models not depending back on features.

The detected cycle is `src/routeTree.gen.ts -> src/router.tsx`, but `routeTree.gen.ts` is explicitly TanStack Router generated code and imports `getRouter` only as a type, so this is a framework-generated typing artifact rather than a confirmed runtime architecture defect.

There are two substantial application/backend trees: root `src` plus `server`, and nested `alb_sheshi/frontend` plus `alb_sheshi/server`; both have package/project files and runnable-looking entry points.

The duplicated server trees are divergent, not archival mirrors: `alb_sheshi/server/Sheshi.Api/Program.cs` adds rate limiting, forwarded headers, JWT validation, production migration gating, and `RoomService`, while root `server/Sheshi.Api/Program.cs` lacks those changes.

The duplicated frontend trees are different architectures: root `src` is a TanStack Start app split into routes/components/lib/hooks, while `alb_sheshi/frontend/src/App.tsx` is a standalone Vite React app with a single large app shell and separate `api.ts` client.
The active backend under server/Sheshi.Api is mostly decomposed by recognizable responsibilities: Auth, Data, Domain, Email, Features, Realtime, and Storage.
Backend feature areas such as server/Sheshi.Api/Features/Messages, Features/Rooms, and Features/Moderation keep controllers, DTOs, and service logic near their domain capability.
The frontend root src/ has a clear route/component/lib/hook split, and TanStack route files map directly to product routes like dhoma.$slug.tsx and tema.$messageId.tsx.
The repository also contains alb_sheshi/, which has its own frontend/package.json, frontend/src, server/Sheshi.sln, server/README.md, docker-compose.yml, and a divergent copy of the API.

diff -qr server alb_sheshi/server shows many production-source differences, including Program.cs, AuthController.cs, AppDbContext.cs, MessageService.cs, RoomsController.cs, ModerationController.cs, migrations, and tests.

The root package.json still names the frontend package tanstack_start_ts while alb_sheshi/frontend/package.json names another frontend package alb-sheshi-frontend, making the public project surface ambiguous.
docs/TODO.md says Supabase runtime was removed and documents a root server/frontend workflow, but it does not explain whether alb_sheshi is archival, alternate production, or stale migration output.

Root frontend modules follow predictable TanStack conventions: route files export `Route = createFileRoute(...)`, feature components use named component exports, and shared UI primitives live under `src/components/ui`.

The root backend uses a clear feature-folder convention under `server/Sheshi.Api/Features/{Messages,Rooms,Moderation}` with sibling `*Controller.cs`, `*Dtos.cs`, and service files where needed.
A second production-shaped tree exists under `alb_sheshi` with its own frontend package and server solution; many files duplicate root paths but differ in behavior and structure.
`server/README.md` instructs developers to run `dotnet run --project server/Sheshi.Api`, while `alb_sheshi/server/Sheshi.Api` also contains controllers, migrations, tests, and app settings.

src/lib/api-client.ts centralizes HTTP failure handling: api() throws ApiError for non-2xx responses, toApiError preserves status and parsed payload when possible, and apiJson/apiForm build on that single boundary.
src/lib/sheshi.ts deliberately maps 404 lookups to null in getRoomBySlug(), getMessage(), and getThread(), which makes not-found reads predictable at the domain layer.

src/server.ts and src/start.ts use top-level SSR error boundaries that log unexpected failures and return a consistent rendered error page, including a special normalization path for h3-swallowed HTTPError responses.

cross_module_architecture.dimension_character: Inside each individual implementation, dependency direction is generally understandable: UI flows down into shared clients, and API controllers/services point inward toward data and domain types. The repo-level architecture is much harder for a new developer to trust because two parallel frontend/backend implementations coexist and have drifted in behavior, security posture, migrations, and API client shape.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: This is above the mixed/60 anchor because the local module boundaries are mostly coherent and the only mechanical cycle is generated framework code. It lands below the solid/80 anchor because the duplicated active-looking roots create codebase-wide boundary ambiguity: common backend or frontend changes could easily be made in the wrong tree or only one of two divergent trees.

high_level_elegance.dimension_character: Inside the active backend, ownership is fairly coherent: domain entities, feature controllers, realtime, storage, auth, and data all have understandable homes. The frontend is also navigable at a first pass, but the repository-level structure is undermined by a second full application tree under alb_sheshi that overlaps the same backend and frontend responsibilities while diverging from the root copies.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: This lands in the mixed-to-solid range: a developer working only in server/ and src/ can usually predict where to put changes, which pushes the score above significant drag. The duplicate alb_sheshi application root and stale public metadata make the top-level ownership contract hard to trust, so the codebase falls well short of the 90 anchor where each top-level package has an obvious, reliable purpose.

convention_outlier.dimension_character: Within the current root app, conventions are mostly coherent: React routes, feature components, shadcn UI primitives, and ASP.NET feature folders each have recognizable local patterns. The main developer-facing problem is that a parallel `alb_sheshi` application tree duplicates the same domains with different implementation conventions, making it unclear which tree is canonical when searching or extending behavior.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: This is better than a fragmented codebase because the active root areas are internally consistent and the mechanical UI naming signal is explainable by shadcn conventions. It lands below the solid 80 anchor because the duplicated app/server roots create recurring navigation ambiguity across major production-looking areas, so developers cannot fully trust that one observed pattern holds everywhere.

error_consistency.dimension_character: The codebase has a reasonably coherent HTTP boundary: API calls throw ApiError, not-found reads become null in a small set of read helpers, and top-level SSR failures are intentionally contained. The weaker part is the migrated frontend surface, where background loads often collapse failures into empty UI states and domain mutations sometimes downgrade structured ApiError context into bare Error message sentinels.
error_consistency.score_rationale: This is better than the 60 anchor because a developer can learn the main API strategy quickly and many user-triggered mutations do surface errors through toasts or inline messages. It falls below the 80 anchor because related data-loading paths still mix silent swallowing, null-as-not-found, empty arrays, and generic toasts, so a developer cannot reliably predict whether a failed request will be visible, recoverable, or indistinguishable from empty data.

naming_quality.dimension_character: Names generally let a new developer predict behavior quickly: frontend domain wrappers use list/get/create/post/toggle vocabulary, backend controllers mirror HTTP actions, and tree/thread helpers are concrete. The main naming weakness is isolated local UI handler naming, not a systemic vocabulary problem.
naming_quality.score_rationale: This sits around the 90 anchor: a developer would trust most names, with only minor friction from local generic verbs. The mechanical filename concern does not lower the score because the kebab-case files are shadcn/Radix compound component conventions, not project-specific naming drift.

abstraction_fitness.dimension_character: The TypeScript codebase is mostly direct and uses abstractions where they clarify boundaries: API helpers add auth, serialization, retry, validation, and domain-level return contracts, while UI wrappers centralize styling or required framework composition. There is little evidence of one-implementation interface ceremony, delegation-heavy class ecosystems, or facade modules that force developers through unpaid indirection.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: This lands in the strong range because a developer can usually see the value of each layer and mechanical concern signals mostly resolve to framework boundaries or deliberately useful shells. It falls short of exemplary because some surfaces, especially the older alb_sheshi frontend App.tsx and a few thin endpoint helpers, lean on direct monolithic composition or light typed shells rather than consistently crisp, feature-sized abstractions.

dependency_health.dimension_character: The main dependency set has a recognizable purpose, but it has drift from a focused application dependency list into a stocked template kit. A developer would mostly understand the chosen stack, yet would hit friction deciding which package root is authoritative and which UI/form/chart dependencies are actually part of the app.
dependency_health.score_rationale: This sits below the solid 80 anchor because the drift is recurring: unused direct dependencies, many unused shadcn/Radix component dependencies, and a second overlapping frontend package manifest create real maintenance ambiguity. It is not near the 60 anchor because the core runtime stack is still coherent and the only detected cycle is a generated type-only TanStack Router artifact rather than a meaningful runtime cycle.

low_level_elegance.dimension_character: The backend and the newer src frontend generally serve developers well at the function level: guard clauses are common, helpers are local, and state mutation is usually explicit. issues.summary: Two divergent app/backend trees make ownership and change targets ambiguous

Root `src/lib/sheshi.ts` exposes API helpers used by route modules; `alb_sheshi/frontend/src/api.ts` exposes a separate API surface with different pagination/thread types, so frontend/backend contract changes must be duplicated or reconciled manually.

issues.summary: Background fetch failures are repeatedly converted into empty or not-found UI states

issues.suggestion: Choose one canonical application tree, then remove or archive the other outside the production source tree. If alb_sheshi is the newer target, move its missing backend/frontend changes into the root tree and delete the duplicate packages; if the root tree is canonical, delete alb_sheshi or mark it explicitly out of scope so future migrations and API changes have one place to land.
evidence: Root package.json exposes the root app as the normal Vite/TanStack surface with dev/build scripts at lines 6-12, while alb_sheshi/frontend/package.json exposes a separate frontend with its own dev/build scripts at lines 6-9.

src/lib/api-client.ts defines the root API contract as api/apiJson/apiForm with stored bearer tokens and refresh-on-401 at lines 22-88; alb_sheshi/frontend/src/api.ts defines a separate endpoint object whose methods require tokens to be passed at call sites at lines 47-79.

server/Sheshi.Api/Domain/Message.cs lacks RootMessageId and Depth, while alb_sheshi/server/Sheshi.Api/Domain/Message.cs includes both fields; alb_sheshi/server/Sheshi.Api/Migrations/20260608125359_ThreadMetadata.cs adds the corresponding columns and indexes, and no matching migration exists under server/Sheshi.Api/Migrations.

issues.summary: A divergent alb_sheshi subtree duplicates the canonical frontend/backend layout
issues.suggestion: Stage the reorg around one canonical app. First decide whether root src/ and server/ are authoritative, which the docs already imply. Move any still-needed alb_sheshi/frontend behavior into target folders under root src/ only: route views into src/routes, shared product components into src/components, API/auth/realtime helpers into src/lib and src/hooks, and strings into src/i18n. Then reconcile backend-only deltas from alb_sheshi/server into server/ feature folders, preserving server/Sheshi.Api as the only backend project. After parity is verified, delete or archive alb_sheshi/ outside the production tree and update package scripts/docs to mention only root npm run build, npm run dev -- --host localhost --port 3001, dotnet test from server, and dotnet run --project server/Sheshi.Api. Validate with npm run build, npm run lint, cd server && dotnet test, and rg "alb_sheshi|frontend/src/App|frontend/src/api" docs package.json server src to catch stale references.
evidence: docs/sheshi-dotnet-backend-master-spec.md lines 121-131 documents the repo layout as root src/ for the React app and root server/ for the .NET backend.

alb_sheshi/frontend/src/App.tsx lines 1-45 define a separate standalone React application with local route parsing and app-wide state instead of using the root src/routes, src/components, src/hooks, and src/lib layout.

alb_sheshi/frontend/src/api.ts lines 1-87 defines a separate API client and route helpers for the same /api/rooms, /api/messages, /api/auth, /api/highlights surface that root src/lib/sheshi.ts and src/lib/api-client.ts own.
diff -qr server alb_sheshi/server reports many differing production files, including AuthController.cs, MessageService.cs, ModerationController.cs, Program.cs, migrations, README.md, and tests.

issues.summary: SSR error capture depends on importing a module for global listener registration
issues.suggestion: Replace the side-effect import with an explicit installErrorCapture() export from src/lib/error-capture.ts, call it once from src/server.ts before getServerEntry() can import the TanStack server entry, and keep consumeLastCapturedError as the read API. This preserves behavior while making the boot-order requirement visible at the call site.
evidence: src/server.ts:1 imports ./lib/error-capture solely for side effects, then imports consumeLastCapturedError from the same module at src/server.ts:3.
src/lib/error-capture.ts:11-16 registers globalThis error and unhandledrejection listeners at module scope.
src/lib/error-capture.ts:4-8 stores the most recent captured error in a single module-level lastCapturedError slot.

issues.summary: API startup always performs database migration and seed work before serving requests
issues.suggestion: Gate MigrateAsync behind a Database:AutoMigrate setting or development/test environment check, while keeping idempotent seed behavior explicit. The alternate alb_sheshi/server/Sheshi.Api/Program.cs:218-231 already shows the intended pattern: dev migrates by default and production opts in.
evidence: server/Sheshi.Api/Program.cs:144-151 creates a service scope, resolves AppDbContext, runs db.Database.MigrateAsync(), and calls DbSeeder.SeedAsync(app.Services) on every host boot.
server/Sheshi.Api.Tests/ApiFactory.cs:9-12 documents that tests rely on the app startup pipeline applying migrations and seeding automatically, so host construction is coupled to a writable database.

issues.summary: Large unused chart component adds unearned UI surface
issues.suggestion: Delete src/components/ui/chart.tsx and the unused Recharts dependency path if no near-term chart feature owns it; otherwise add the feature import that makes this component part of the active product surface.
evidence: Repository search found no imports of src/components/ui/chart.tsx, while the file defines ChartContainer, ChartStyle, ChartTooltipContent, ChartLegendContent, and Recharts-specific payload helpers.
The app has no visible chart feature in the inspected route files, so this module reads as retained scaffold rather than an earned abstraction.

issues.summary: Large unused sidebar module obscures the actual navigation design
issues.suggestion: Remove src/components/ui/sidebar.tsx if the app has standardized on AppShell, or wire AppShell to this sidebar system and delete the competing unused navigation primitives.
evidence: Repository search found no imports of src/components/ui/sidebar.tsx.
The file is 744 lines and owns provider state, cookies, keyboard shortcuts, mobile Sheet behavior, layout primitives, menu variants, actions, badges, skeletons, and submenus.
The active app navigation appears to use AppShell/route-level layout rather than this sidebar system, so a new developer has to determine whether this large component family is real or leftover.

issues.summary: Legacy App.tsx mixes application orchestration and most views in one file
issues.suggestion: Split along existing boundaries without adding new abstractions: move route/view components such as Home, RoomView, ThreadView, AuthPage, and Profile into focused sibling files, and keep App.tsx responsible for top-level state orchestration until repeated state patterns justify hooks.
evidence: App() owns route parsing, history navigation, theme persistence, auth persistence, provider loading, callback handling, room/highlight/presence loading, SignalR setup, room message paging, thread loading, optimistic voting, saving, sharing, message submission, auth modal state, create-room modal state, and toast state.

The same file also defines TopBar, RoomRail, Home, RoomView, ThreadView, ThreadCard, ActionRow, ReplyTree, Composer, ReplyComposer, FocusPanel, AuthPage, Profile, CreateRoomDialog, Dialog, and data-ranking helpers.
A new team member cannot understand ownership in 30 seconds because changing one workflow requires scanning a 1360-line module with both app orchestration and UI implementation.

issues.reasoning: The setState-only effect is a purposeful reset of local optimistic UI after the backing message changes, not an avoidable responsibility split.
The module has a clear transport responsibility, and Promise-returning wrappers are an acceptable API shape here.
The async wrapper pattern is consistent and simpler than adding extra service objects or dependency wrappers.
The function is long but has one coherent route responsibility, which the dimension rubric explicitly says to skip.
The setState-only effect supports a form draft reset and does not indicate mixed responsibilities.
The component is close to the design boundary, but the responsibilities are all route-local and understandable after one pass.
The file is used through framework configuration rather than imports, and its body has one clear responsibility.
Generated framework output is not an authored mixed-responsibility module.

issues.summary: apiJson<T> can return undefined despite promising Promise<T>
issues.suggestion: Split the contract into explicit helpers, for example keep apiJson<T>() for JSON responses that must parse a body and add apiNoContent() or apiJson<T | void>() overloads for 204-capable endpoints. Remove the blanket undefined as T cast so non-void callers cannot silently accept no-content responses.
evidence: src/lib/api-client.ts:49 declares export async function apiJson<T>(...): Promise<T>.
src/lib/api-client.ts:57 returns undefined as T whenever the HTTP response status is 204.
Callers use both apiJson<void> for no-content endpoints and apiJson<ApiUser>/apiJson<Room[]> for data endpoints, so the helper's public contract does not encode which calls may return no body.

issues.summary: Getter-style exports hide initialization side effects
issues.suggestion: Rename the side-effecting entry points to make initialization explicit, such as ensureRealtimeConnection() and initializeAuthAndGetSnapshot(), or split each into a pure peek/get function plus an ensure/init function used by callers that want construction.
evidence: src/lib/realtime.ts:7 exports getRealtimeConnection(), but lines 10-15 construct and cache a new HubConnection on the first call.
src/hooks/use-auth.ts:87 exports getAuthSnapshot(), but line 88 calls init(), and init() at lines 55-61 can start loadUserFromTokens() and subscribe to token-store changes.
Pure getters elsewhere, such as src/lib/token-store.ts:13 getStoredTokens(), only read and return state, so these exported get* APIs are surprising by comparison.

issues.summary: Thread reload can apply a stale getThread result after navigation changes messageId
issues.suggestion: Move reload into the effect or pass it a request token/cancelled flag, and check that flag before setThread, lastReplyIdRef writes, scroll scheduling, and setLoading(false).
evidence: reload() closes over messageId and calls getThread(messageId).then(... setThread(data) ...) at lines 70-90.

The effect at lines 106-129 reruns for joinedThreadId/messageId/userId and sets disposed only for realtime handler attachment/removal; reload() itself has no disposed/request-id check before setThread(data).
A similar route fetch in src/routes/dhoma.$slug.tsx lines 49-65 uses a cancelled flag before setting state, so this route is an outlier from an existing safer pattern.

issues.summary: Overlapping auth loads can restore a user after tokens were replaced or cleared
issues.suggestion: Capture the access token used for the load and, after await, compare it with the current stored token before setting user; ignore stale completions or use a monotonically increasing load id.
evidence: loadUserFromTokens() captures tokens at lines 38-45, awaits apiJson('/api/me') at line 47, then writes user state using getStoredTokens() at line 48 without verifying the stored tokens still match the request that just completed.

clearStoredTokens() and setStoredTokens() synchronously emit token-store listeners at src/lib/token-store.ts lines 26-35, which can start a newer loadUserFromTokens while an older one is still in flight.
If an older /api/me request resolves after signOutLocal() or another token update, it can set { session: null, user, isReady: true }, producing an inconsistent authenticated user with no session.

issues.summary: API facade wrappers are marked async even when they only return another promise
issues.suggestion: Remove async from the pure pass-through wrappers and return Promise<T> directly, reserving async for functions that await to transform errors or branch on results.
evidence: listRooms(), createRoom(), listMessages(), listReplies(), and listHighlights are declared async but their bodies only return apiJson(...) directly at lines 45-46, 58-65, 68-69, 81-82, and 160-161.

These functions do not contain framework-required async signatures or local try/catch logic; neighboring functions such as getRoomBySlug() and getMessage() use async because they actually await to translate 404s to null.
Mechanical concern confirmed: smells::src/lib/sheshi.ts::async_no_await.

issues.summary: Root frontend API models mark server-guaranteed response fields as optional
issues.suggestion: Tighten src/lib/sheshi.ts to match the server DTOs: make Room.thread_count required, latest_activity_at present nullable, and MessageRow.author/upvotes/reply_count/voted required. Keep null only where the backend record is nullable.
evidence: src/lib/sheshi.ts lines 8-9 declare Room.thread_count? and latest_activity_at?, while server/Sheshi.Api/Features/Rooms/RoomDtos.cs lines 3-9 returns ThreadCount as a required int and LatestActivityAt as a present nullable DateTimeOffset.

src/lib/sheshi.ts lines 28-31 declare MessageRow.author?, upvotes?, reply_count?, and voted?, while server/Sheshi.Api/Features/Messages/MessageDtos.cs lines 16-19 returns Author, Upvotes, ReplyCount, and Voted as present DTO fields.
alb_sheshi/frontend/src/types.ts lines 17-24 and 33-48 show the migrated frontend already models the same fields as required, so the root frontend type is a stale looser variant.

issues.summary: Moderation report DTO weakens domain enums into raw strings
issues.suggestion: Change ModReportDto.Reason to ReportReason and Status to ReportStatus, import Sheshi.Api.Domain in ModerationDtos.cs, and pass r.Reason/r.Status directly from ModerationController.
evidence: server/Sheshi.Api/Domain/Enums.cs defines ReportReason and ReportStatus, but server/Sheshi.Api/Features/Moderation/ModerationDtos.cs lines 7 and 9 expose Reason and Status as string.
server/Sheshi.Api/Features/Moderation/ModerationController.cs lines 30-32 manually projects r.Reason and r.Status through ToString().ToLowerInvariant(), bypassing the enum-typed API shape.
server/Sheshi.Api/Program.cs line 27 already registers JsonStringEnumConverter(JsonNamingPolicy.SnakeCaseLower), so enum-typed DTO properties would preserve the same snake-case wire format.

add.description: The root frontend is easy to reason about within its own tree because route files consume components and shared lib helpers, while shared helpers do not depend back on route modules. This gives local changes a predictable direction despite the broader repo duplication.

The API projects generally keep controllers, services, data, domain, storage, auth, and realtime in recognizable buckets. Controllers depend on services/data/domain, while domain types stay free of feature/controller dependencies.

The reported route tree cycle comes from TanStack Router generated code and a type-only reference to the router factory, so it is better treated as framework noise than as a hand-authored boundary violation.

Root and nested frontend/backend trees are both runnable-looking but contain different architecture and behavior. This makes the true ownership boundary unclear and raises the chance of common changes landing in only one implementation.

The active API tree has predictable domain ownership: Auth owns identity flows, Features owns product endpoints, Realtime owns SignalR/presence, Storage owns image persistence, and Domain owns entities. That structure lets backend changes stay mostly inside the capability they affect.

The active frontend route files map cleanly to visible product areas such as rooms, threads, auth, moderation, profile, focus, and sitemap. This gives a new engineer a useful first navigation model before reading component internals.

The repository contains a second full application tree with its own frontend, backend, infra, docs, and divergent server code. Because it is not excluded or clearly labeled archival, it weakens trust in which top-level packages are authoritative.

The root package name and available docs do not form one clear contract for the current Sheshi app layout. This makes runtime reality discoverable only after comparing package files, READMEs, and duplicate directories.

The current `src` frontend separates file routes, feature components, hooks, library API helpers, and shadcn UI primitives in predictable places. This lets developers transfer patterns across route and component modules without learning a new local style per file.

The kebab-case compound filenames under `src/components/ui` come from the configured shadcn primitive layer, where multi-word component families naturally use names like `alert-dialog` and `dropdown-menu`. Treating that as naming drift would push against the external convention developers already expect.

add.header: Parallel app roots obscure canonical code
add.description: The repository contains both root `src`/`server` code and an `alb_sheshi` app/server tree with overlapping domains but divergent implementation patterns. Because both trees look production-shaped and differ behaviorally, developers must inspect project wiring before trusting a convention they find.

add.header: Backend feature folders are locally predictable
add.description: Inside the root backend, feature folders group controllers, DTOs, and services by domain instead of scattering related API code across unrelated directories. This supports navigation once the developer is in the canonical `server/Sheshi.Api` tree.

HTTP failures pass through a single api() wrapper that throws ApiError with status and parsed payload where available. This gives most service calls a predictable failure shape before route/component code starts adapting it.

Read helpers for room, message, and thread lookup intentionally convert 404 ApiError responses to null. That convention is understandable because callers render not-found states for those resource lookups.

Server startup and request middleware handle unexpected SSR failures at the top level, including a documented h3 swallowed-error normalization path. This is a deliberate boundary rather than accidental catch-and-hide behavior.

Many non-form data loads treat failed requests as empty optional data, which makes outages and permission problems look like normal empty states. This creates the main unpredictability in developer-facing error behavior.

Some domain helpers translate structured ApiError failures into plain Error messages such as UNAUTH and TOO_LONG. That simplifies local UI checks but loses status, payload, and cause context across the boundary.

The frontend Sheshi API wrapper consistently uses list/get/create/post/toggle/softDelete/submit names that map to user-visible domain actions. This lets callers infer both resource and behavior without opening the helper.
Controller methods use conventional HTTP-domain names such as ListRoomMessages, GetMessage, GetThread, and PostMessage. The names line up with route attributes and make endpoint intent easy to scan.

Multiword files in src/components/ui use kebab-case because they mirror shadcn/Radix component naming. The convention is localized and recognizable, so it helps navigation more than it harms consistency.

The main HTTP client and domain API module add auth refresh, JSON/FormData translation, error normalization, validation, and null-on-404 contracts, so callers are shielded from backend protocol details without navigating empty pass-through wrappers.

The single-use route, generated route tree, server entry, and root toaster files correspond to framework lifecycle and composition boundaries. Their one-import shape does not indicate avoidable abstraction ceremony.

The inspected TypeScript uses concrete exported shapes and component props instead of broad one-implementation service interfaces or delegation-heavy classes. Developers do not have to chase interface ecosystems to find runtime behavior.

alb_sheshi/frontend/src/App.tsx concentrates many concerns in one component, which is a decomposition issue more than an abstraction-fitness defect. It reduces indirection, but future changes may pressure extraction into earned feature modules.

The live application centers on React 19, TanStack Start/Router/Query, Tailwind 4, SignalR, and Radix-backed shadcn primitives, so the main technology choices are understandable despite cleanup needs.

The mechanical cycle is from TanStack Router's generated routeTree.gen.ts importing getRouter only as an import type for module augmentation. That does not create runtime circular loading risk and should not drive dependency-health scoring.

The package manifest still carries many Radix/shadcn companion packages because unused generated UI wrapper files remain in src/components/ui. This makes dependency intent harder to read and keeps the installed tree broader than the app behavior requires.

The root app and alb_sheshi/frontend each maintain package.json and package-lock.json with overlapping frontend dependencies but different declared ranges. Without a workspace or single authoritative manifest, future installs can resolve different tool and runtime versions.

add.header: Backend helpers stay local and direct
add.description: Controller and service helpers are extracted at the point where they simplify one operation: DTO enrichment, room normalization, thread traversal, and request parsing. That keeps control flow readable without creating a separate abstraction layer to navigate.

The current src/lib/sheshi.ts functions mostly do one transport operation plus the smallest needed normalization. This gives callers predictable behavior without forcing them through broad client abstractions.

The older alb_sheshi frontend has not been reduced to the cleaner component and hook style used in the current src app. Its internals combine side effects, rendering, optimistic state, and repeated interaction controls, so low-level readability depends heavily on which tree a developer is editing.

add.header: Frontend API boundary is mostly centralized
add.description: The api-client and sheshi modules give the frontend one main place for HTTP mechanics, token refresh, DTO typing, and domain-level calls. This makes most page-to-backend handoffs explicit and unsurprising.

Program.cs applies snake_case and enum JSON policy globally, so controllers and DTO records do not need ad-hoc serialization mapping at each endpoint. That keeps the backend/frontend DTO boundary easy to reason about.

add.header: Message enrichment has an obvious collaborator
add.description: MessageService owns author, vote, reply count, and caller-voted enrichment across several message endpoints. The pattern reduces repeated mapper queries at the controller boundary.

The shared realtime module creates the connection but leaves event names, join/leave method names, startup handling, and cleanup choreography to route components. This makes the route-to-realtime seam harder to understand and change than the HTTP API seam.

MessageController coordinates persistence, validation, storage, thread traversal, enrichment, and notification while MessageService handles only enrichment. The HTTP layer therefore owns too much cross-module workflow state.

add.header: Backend integration tests use real boundaries
add.description: The ASP.NET test suite boots the application through WebApplicationFactory against Testcontainers PostgreSQL, so the tests validate HTTP routing, EF mappings, migrations, seeded roles, auth, and persistence together. This gives backend developers meaningful confidence without coupling tests to controller internals.

Existing server tests exercise auth lifecycle, profile updates, rooms, messages, replies, voting, reports, highlights, moderation, SignalR notifications, presence, and multipart uploads. The coverage is concentrated on behavior users and API clients depend on.
The current test portfolio is assertion-based HTTP and model verification, not snapshot-driven. That avoids the common fragility where large serialized outputs become the main oracle.

add.header: Frontend has no automated test layer
add.description: The React client contains stateful API, auth, realtime, route, and component behavior but the package has no test script or frontend testing dependencies. Developers changing the client must rely on build/lint and manual exploration rather than automated regression checks.

add.header: Generated router output should be skipped
add.description: src/routeTree.gen.ts is generated TanStack router output, so its detector signal is not strategically important coverage debt. Tests should target routes and navigation behavior, not the generated tree file itself.

The main src/lib/sheshi.ts client surface gives developers a regular async vocabulary for rooms, messages, threads, votes, deletes, reports, and highlights. Most operations return typed Promises and keep create/post/report payloads in input objects rather than long parameter lists.

src/lib/api-client.ts gives raw, JSON, and multipart calls distinct helper names, which keeps most endpoint wrappers from hand-rolling fetch behavior. That separation makes the common JSON path predictable while still leaving an escape hatch for file upload and raw no-content responses.

The newer alb_sheshi server services use explicit result records for create/delete/report outcomes, making controller failure handling visible without overloading null or exception behavior. This improves API honesty at the service-controller boundary.

The repository carries root and alb_sheshi client/server surfaces for the same room/message domain, and those surfaces have diverged on pagination envelopes, DTO fields, and token placement. Developers have to check which copy they are touching before assuming the contract.

Message, room, and moderation writes in the evolved API copy use server-side authorization checks and integration tests exercise unauthenticated, regular, moderator, admin, and banned-user cases. This gives developers a real contract to extend rather than relying on frontend-only checks.

The repository keeps two server implementations with divergent authorization behavior while documentation still points at the top-level server path. That migration residue makes it unclear which protected-resource pattern a developer should copy.

Backend role constants exist, but some Authorize attributes and frontend gates still spell roles as literals. This is not currently a broad security hole, but it leaves permission vocabulary vulnerable to drift.

add.header: Core app code is low ceremony
add.description: The main route, component, and API helper files are generally terse and focused, with few comments and no broad debug logging. Developers spend most attention on UI and network behavior rather than filtering boilerplate prose.

add.header: Boundary error handling is mostly justified
add.description: The repeated try/catch shape appears around fetch calls, auth failures, optimistic UI rollback, browser APIs, and SSR failure normalization. Those are genuine API or runtime boundaries, so the pattern should not be treated as pure defensive overengineering.

High-comment files such as config.server.ts and error-capture.ts explain Cloudflare env binding, client/server secret exposure, and h3 SSR error behavior. These comments preserve operational context that is not obvious from the code alone.

The UI directory still carries many unused shadcn-style wrappers, including large orphaned files with copied explanatory comments. This makes search and navigation noisier than the actual product surface requires.

add.header: Supabase removal has a documented target
add.description: The docs identify the intended migration direction and record Supabase SDK/runtime wiring removal, so the old platform migration is not completely untracked. That lowers ambiguity around the intended backend direction even though duplicate app trees remain.

add.header: Parallel runnable trees blur migration ownership
add.description: Both root and alb_sheshi contain runnable frontend/backend source with different API-client shapes and backend schema states. This makes future work structurally ambiguous because developers have to decide which tree owns a migration fix before they can safely change behavior.

The objective coupling matrix supports the root src/ split: routes depend on product components, UI primitives, hooks, i18n, and lib, while shared lib code is consumed broadly with high fan-in. This gives developers a useful default path for finding route behavior versus shared infrastructure.

src/components/ui is large, but components.json explicitly points the shadcn ui alias at that folder and the files are cohesive primitive wrappers. The flat directory is less navigationally ideal at 46 files, but it is not strong evidence of mixed ownership.

The documented layout names root src/ and server/ as canonical, yet alb_sheshi contains another frontend and divergent server copy. That makes ownership hard to infer from paths and invites fixes to land in the wrong implementation.

add.header: Config reads avoid stale env snapshots
add.description: Server config and API base URL are exposed as getter functions rather than module-level captured constants, which keeps imports cheap and avoids fragile env timing in tests and worker-style runtimes.
Token persistence reads localStorage only inside functions with typeof window checks, so SSR imports do not immediately touch browser-only APIs.
getRouter() constructs QueryClient and router together instead of exporting a shared singleton, reducing cross-request and cross-test state bleed.
PresenceTracker is a singleton, but it is registered through ASP.NET DI and protects internal dictionaries with a lock and snapshot copies, making the shared state boundary explicit.
The error recovery path depends on importing a module that installs global listeners at module scope, so the required boot order is real but hidden in import syntax.

add.header: Database work is embedded in API boot
add.description: The primary server host runs migrations and seeding during startup, which makes host construction depend on database availability and migration permissions before the app can serve requests.

The current src/routes files generally keep one route workflow per file and use small local helpers where that improves scanability. This gives developers a clear place to make page-level changes without extra ceremony.

api-client owns transport concerns while sheshi.ts owns domain endpoint names and types. The split is direct enough that developers can predict where token refresh, 404 handling, and message operations live.

alb_sheshi/frontend/src/App.tsx concentrates orchestration, routing, data loading, realtime behavior, and nearly all views in one module. That makes the older frontend much harder to explain or modify than the current route-based src tree.

Large ui modules such as sidebar and chart are present without importers, so the component folder advertises capabilities the product does not currently use. This creates design noise even when the active components are straightforward.

add.header: Generated route tree is detector noise
add.description: routeTree.gen.ts is generated TanStack Router wiring and should not be judged like authored design code. Its casts, ts-nocheck, and single importer are framework output rather than a signal to refactor.
The main domain read helpers make the 404-to-null behavior explicit in their return types, so callers can distinguish absence from other API failures without reading every implementation.
The token-store module keeps read, write, clear, and subscribe responsibilities separated behind names that accurately describe their side effects.
The shared API and singleton/auth helpers favor convenience over precise contracts, which is why their signatures and getter names hide important runtime behavior at subsystem boundaries.

add.header: Guard clauses keep route states readable
add.description: Primary UI routes tend to use explicit loading, empty, not-found, and auth guard branches, so ordinary render flow is easy to trace without reconstructing nested conditionals.

Some route fetches protect state writes after parameter changes, while thread and auth flows still allow older async completions to win; developers cannot assume route loaders share the same concurrency invariant.

add.header: API facade mixes awaited and pass-through async
add.description: The API layer uses async appropriately for error translation paths, but several exported wrappers are async only by declaration, which adds small ambiguity about where asynchronous branching actually happens.

add.header: Strict compilers on both active stacks
add.description: The active TypeScript app uses strict mode and the .NET API enables nullable reference types, and both production builds complete cleanly. This gives developers a solid baseline where most type annotations are enforced rather than decorative.

add.header: DTO records model most API contracts
add.description: The backend generally uses explicit request and response records instead of loose dictionaries or dynamic payloads. That keeps controller contracts readable and lets nullable and enum decisions stay visible at API boundaries.

The root frontend and alb_sheshi frontend carry separate hand-written API model definitions, and the migrated copy is stricter for several message and room fields. This creates a type-safety maintenance risk because one side can silently preserve stale optionality.

add.header: Moderation enums are weakened at DTO edge
add.description: Moderation report output converts domain enums into strings even though global JSON options already support enum string serialization. That small boundary shortcut makes the API contract less precise than the domain model.
